questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: When a person departs his home for sightseeing by his car, the meter reads $12352 \text{ km}$. Upon returning home after two hours, the reading is $12416 \text{ km}$. What is the average speed of the car during this period in km/h?
questions.solution:
Initial reading of the meter $= 12352 \text{ km}$

Final reading of the meter $= 12416 \text{ km}$

Total distance travelled by the car $= 12416 - 12352 = 64 \text{ km}$

Total time taken $= 2 \text{ hours}$

Average speed $= \dfrac{\text{Total distance}}{\text{Total time}}$

Average speed $= \dfrac{64}{2} = 32 \text{ km/h}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A man has to travel $50 \text{ m}$ due north, $40 \text{ m}$ due east, and $20 \text{ m}$ due south to reach a field from his house. Determine his displacement from the house to the field.
questions.solution:
Let the starting point be the origin $(0,0)$. Taking East along the positive x-axis and North along the positive y-axis, the individual displacements are:

$\vec{d}_1 = 50 \hat{j}$

$\vec{d}_2 = 40 \hat{i}$

$\vec{d}_3 = -20 \hat{j}$

The net displacement vector is:

$\vec{D} = \vec{d}_1 + \vec{d}_2 + \vec{d}_3 = 40 \hat{i} + (50 - 20) \hat{j} = 40 \hat{i} + 30 \hat{j}$

The magnitude of the displacement is:

$|\vec{D}| = \sqrt{40^2 + 30^2} = \sqrt{1600 + 900} = 50 \text{ m}$

The angle $\theta$ made with the East direction is:

$\tan \theta = \dfrac{y}{x} = \dfrac{30}{40} = \dfrac{3}{4}$

$\theta = \tan^{-1}(3/4)$ North of East.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A man has to travel $50 \text{ m}$ due north, $40 \text{ m}$ due east, and $20 \text{ m}$ due south to reach a field. Determine the distance he has to walk to reach the field in m.
questions.solution:
Distance is the total path length covered by the person.

The man travels $50 \text{ m}$ north, $40 \text{ m}$ east, and $20 \text{ m}$ south.

Total distance walked $= 50 + 40 + 20 = 110 \text{ m}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: When a person departs his home for sightseeing by his car, the meter reads $12352 \text{ km}$. Upon returning home after two hours, the reading is $12416 \text{ km}$. What is the average velocity of the car during this period in km/h?
questions.solution:
Since the person departs from his home and returns to the same place, the initial and final positions are identical.

Net displacement of the car = $0$

Total time taken = $2 \text{ hours}$

Average velocity = $\dfrac{\text{Net displacement}}{\text{Total time}}$

Average velocity = $\dfrac{0}{2} = 0 \text{ km/h}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An athlete requires $2.0 \text{ s}$ to reach his maximum speed of $18.0 \text{ km/h}$. Determine the magnitude of his average acceleration in $\text{m/s}^2$.
questions.solution:
Initial velocity, $u = 0$

Final velocity, $v = 18.0 \text{ km/h} = 18.0 \times \dfrac{5}{18} \text{ m/s} = 5.0 \text{ m/s}$

Time taken, $t = 2.0 \text{ s}$

Average acceleration, $a = \dfrac{v - u}{t}$

$a = \dfrac{5.0 - 0}{2.0} = 2.5 \text{ m/s}^2$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The figure displays the speed of a car as a function of time. Determine the distance travelled by the car in $8$ seconds and its acceleration.
questions.solution:
The distance travelled by the car is given by the area under the speed-time graph.

$\text{Distance} = \dfrac{1}{2} \times \text{base} \times \text{height}$

$\text{Distance} = \dfrac{1}{2} \times 8 \times 20 = 80 \text{ m}$

The acceleration of the car is equal to the slope of the speed-time graph.

$\text{Acceleration} = \dfrac{\Delta v}{\Delta t} = \dfrac{20 - 0}{8 - 0} = 2.5 \text{ m/s}^2$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Starting at $t = 0$, the acceleration of a cart varies with time as shown in the figure. Determine the distance travelled in $30$ seconds and select the correct description of its position-time graph.
options: $500 \text{ ft}$; the position-time graph is parabolic for $0$ to $10 \text{ s}$ and $20$ to $30 \text{ s}$, and a sloped straight line for $10$ to $20 \text{ s}$.
$1000 \text{ ft}$; the position-time graph is parabolic for $0$ to $10 \text{ s}$ and $20$ to $30 \text{ s}$, and a sloped straight line for $10$ to $20 \text{ s}$.
$500 \text{ ft}$; the position-time graph is a sloped straight line for $0$ to $10 \text{ s}$ and $20$ to $30 \text{ s}$, and horizontal for $10$ to $20 \text{ s}$.
$1000 \text{ ft}$; the position-time graph is a sloped straight line for $0$ to $10 \text{ s}$ and $20$ to $30 \text{ s}$, and horizontal for $10$ to $20 \text{ s}$.
questions.solution:
Assuming the cart starts from rest, the initial velocity is $u = 0$.

For the time interval $0 \le t \le 10 \text{ s}$:
Acceleration $a_1 = 5 \text{ ft/s}^2$.
Velocity at $t = 10 \text{ s}$ is $v_1 = u + a_1 t = 0 + 5 \times 10 = 50 \text{ ft/s}$.
Distance travelled $s_1 = \dfrac{1}{2} a_1 t^2 = \dfrac{1}{2} \times 5 \times (10)^2 = 250 \text{ ft}$.
Since acceleration is constant and non-zero, the position-time graph is a parabola.

For the time interval $10 < t \le 20 \text{ s}$:
Acceleration $a_2 = 0 \text{ ft/s}^2$.
The cart moves with a constant velocity $v_2 = 50 \text{ ft/s}$.
Distance travelled $s_2 = v_2 \times \Delta t = 50 \times 10 = 500 \text{ ft}$.
Since velocity is constant, the position-time graph is a sloped straight line.

For the time interval $20 < t \le 30 \text{ s}$:
Acceleration $a_3 = -5 \text{ ft/s}^2$.
Distance travelled $s_3 = v_2 \Delta t + \dfrac{1}{2} a_3 (\Delta t)^2 = 50 \times 10 - \dfrac{1}{2} \times 5 \times (10)^2 = 500 - 250 = 250 \text{ ft}$.
Since acceleration is constant and non-zero, the position-time graph is again a parabola.

Total distance travelled $S = s_1 + s_2 + s_3 = 250 + 500 + 250 = 1000 \text{ ft}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The graph of velocity versus time for a particle going along the X-axis is depicted in the figure. Find the distance travelled in $0$ to $10 \text{ s}$ in meters.
questions.solution:
The distance travelled by the particle is given by the area under the velocity-time graph.

The region under the graph from $t = 0$ to $t = 10\text{ s}$ is a trapezium with parallel sides of lengths $2\text{ m/s}$ and $8\text{ m/s}$, and a height of $10\text{ s}$.

Distance $= \text{Area of trapezium} = \dfrac{1}{2} \times (\text{sum of parallel sides}) \times \text{time interval}$

Distance $= \dfrac{1}{2} \times (2 + 8) \times 10$

Distance $= \dfrac{1}{2} \times 10 \times 10 = 50\text{ m}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The figure displays the graph of the $x$-coordinate of a particle travelling along the X-axis as a function of time. Determine the average velocity of the particle during the interval from $0$ to $10\text{ s}$ in $\text{m/s}$.
questions.solution:
The average velocity of a particle is defined as the total displacement divided by the total time taken.

From the given position-time graph:
At $t = 0\text{ s}$, the initial position $x_1 = 0\text{ m}$.
At $t = 10\text{ s}$, the final position $x_2 = 100\text{ m}$.

The displacement of the particle during this time interval is:
$\Delta x = x_2 - x_1 = 100\text{ m} - 0\text{ m} = 100\text{ m}$

The time interval is:
$\Delta t = 10\text{ s} - 0\text{ s} = 10\text{ s}$

The average velocity $v_{\text{avg}}$ is:
$v_{\text{avg}} = \dfrac{\Delta x}{\Delta t} = \dfrac{100}{10} = 10\text{ m/s}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The velocity versus time graph for a particle going along the X-axis is illustrated in the figure. Calculate the displacement in $0$ to $10 \text{ s}$ in meters.
questions.solution:
The displacement of a particle is equal to the area under its velocity-time graph.

From the given graph, the area under the curve from $t = 0$ to $t = 10 \text{ s}$ forms a trapezium with parallel sides $v = 2 \text{ m/s}$ and $v = 8 \text{ m/s}$, and height $\Delta t = 10 \text{ s}$.

$\text{Displacement} = \text{Area of trapezium}$

$\text{Displacement} = \dfrac{1}{2} \times (\text{sum of parallel sides}) \times \text{time interval}$

$\text{Displacement} = \dfrac{1}{2} \times (2 + 8) \times 10$

$\text{Displacement} = \dfrac{1}{2} \times 10 \times 10 = 50 \text{ m}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The distance from Patna to Ranchi is $260 \text{ km}$ by air and $320 \text{ km}$ by road. An aeroplane requires $30$ minutes to travel from Patna to Ranchi, while a delux bus takes $8$ hours. Determine the average velocity of the bus.
questions.solution:
The average velocity is defined as the ratio of total displacement to the total time taken.

The displacement of the bus is the shortest straight-line distance between the initial and final positions, which is equal to the distance by air.

Displacement $= 260 \text{ km}$

The direction of displacement is from Patna to Ranchi.

Time taken by the bus $= 8 \text{ h}$

Average velocity $= \dfrac{\text{Displacement}}{\text{Time}} = \dfrac{260}{8} = 32.5 \text{ km/h}$

The direction of the average velocity is the same as the direction of displacement, which is from Patna to Ranchi.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The distance from Patna to Ranchi is $260 \text{ km}$ by air and $320 \text{ km}$ by road. An aeroplane requires $30$ minutes to travel from Patna to Ranchi, while a delux bus takes $8$ hours. Determine the average velocity of the plane.
questions.solution:
Displacement of the aeroplane is the straight line distance between Patna and Ranchi, which is $260 \text{ km}$.

Time taken by the aeroplane, $t = 30 \text{ minutes} = \dfrac{1}{2} \text{ h}$.

Average velocity is defined as the ratio of displacement to time taken.

Average velocity $= \dfrac{\text{Displacement}}{t} = \dfrac{260}{\dfrac{1}{2}} = 520 \text{ km/h}$.

The direction of average velocity is along the displacement, which is from Patna to Ranchi.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The distance from Patna to Ranchi is $260 \text{ km}$ by air and $320 \text{ km}$ by road. An aeroplane requires $30$ minutes to travel from Patna to Ranchi, while a delux bus takes $8$ hours. Determine the average speed of the bus in km/h.
questions.solution:
The total distance covered by the bus by road is $d = 320 \text{ km}$.

The total time taken by the bus is $t = 8 \text{ h}$.

The average speed of the bus is given by the ratio of total distance to total time:

$\text{Average speed} = \dfrac{d}{t}$

$\text{Average speed} = \dfrac{320}{8} = 40 \text{ km/h}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The distance from Patna to Ranchi is $260 \text{ km}$ by air and $320 \text{ km}$ by road. An aeroplane requires $30$ minutes to travel from Patna to Ranchi, while a delux bus takes $8$ hours. Determine the average speed of the plane in km/h.
questions.solution:
Distance covered by the aeroplane, $d = 260 \text{ km}$

Time taken by the aeroplane, $t = 30 \text{ minutes} = \dfrac{1}{2} \text{ h}$

Average speed of the plane $= \dfrac{d}{t}$

Average speed $= \dfrac{260}{1/2} = 520 \text{ km/h}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The figure displays the graph of the $x$-coordinate of a particle travelling along the X-axis as a function of time. Determine the instantaneous velocity of the particle at $2\text{ s}$, $5\text{ s}$, $8\text{ s}$, and $12\text{ s}$ respectively.
questions.solution:
The instantaneous velocity of a particle is given by the slope of its position-time ($x-t$) graph, $v = \dfrac{dx}{dt}$.

At $t = 2\text{ s}$, the particle is on the line segment from $t = 0$ to $t = 2.5\text{ s}$. The slope is:
$v_1 = \dfrac{50 - 0}{2.5 - 0} = 20\text{ m/s}$

At $t = 5\text{ s}$, the particle is on the horizontal line segment from $t = 2.5\text{ s}$ to $t = 7.5\text{ s}$. The slope is:
$v_2 = \dfrac{50 - 50}{7.5 - 2.5} = 0$

At $t = 8\text{ s}$, the particle is on the line segment from $t = 7.5\text{ s}$ to $t = 10.0\text{ s}$. The slope is:
$v_3 = \dfrac{100 - 50}{10.0 - 7.5} = \dfrac{50}{2.5} = 20\text{ m/s}$

At $t = 12\text{ s}$, the particle is on the line segment from $t = 10.0\text{ s}$ to $t = 15.0\text{ s}$. The slope is:
$v_4 = \dfrac{0 - 100}{15.0 - 10.0} = \dfrac{-100}{5.0} = -20\text{ m/s}$

Therefore, the instantaneous velocities are $20\text{ m/s}$, zero, $20\text{ m/s}$, and $-20\text{ m/s}$ respectively.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A particle begins at the origin, travels along the X-axis to the point $(20 \text{ m}, 0)$, and subsequently returns along the same line to the point $(-20 \text{ m}, 0)$. Determine the distance and displacement of the particle for this trip.
questions.solution:
Distance is the total path length covered by the particle.

The particle travels from $x = 0$ to $x = 20 \text{ m}$, covering a distance of $20 \text{ m}$.

It then travels from $x = 20 \text{ m}$ to $x = -20 \text{ m}$, covering a distance of $|-20 - 20| = 40 \text{ m}$.

Total distance $= 20 \text{ m} + 40 \text{ m} = 60 \text{ m}$.

Displacement is the change in position of the particle.

Initial position, $x_i = 0 \text{ m}$.

Final position, $x_f = -20 \text{ m}$.

Displacement $= x_f - x_i = -20 \text{ m} - 0 \text{ m} = -20 \text{ m}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Based on the velocity-time plot shown in the figure, determine the distance travelled by the particle during the first $40$ seconds. Also determine the average velocity during this period.
questions.solution:
The distance travelled by the particle is the total area under the velocity-time graph, taking all areas as positive.

Area above the time axis ($A_1$) from $t = 0$ to $t = 20\text{ s}$:
$A_1 = \dfrac{1}{2} \times 20 \times 5 = 50\text{ m}$

Area below the time axis ($A_2$) from $t = 20$ to $t = 40\text{ s}$:
$A_2 = \dfrac{1}{2} \times 20 \times |-5| = 50\text{ m}$

Total distance $= A_1 + A_2 = 50 + 50 = 100\text{ m}$

The displacement is the net area under the velocity-time graph, considering the signs.

Total displacement $= A_1 - A_2 = 50 - 50 = 0\text{ m}$

Average velocity is the total displacement divided by the total time taken.

Average velocity $= \dfrac{\text{Total displacement}}{\text{Total time}} = \dfrac{0}{40} = 0\text{ m/s}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Starting from rest, a train travels with a constant acceleration of $2.0 \text{ m/s}^2$ for half a minute. Subsequently, the brakes are applied, bringing the train to rest in one minute. Determine the total distance moved by the train in km.
questions.solution:
For the acceleration phase:
Initial velocity, $u = 0$
Acceleration, $a_1 = 2.0 \text{ m/s}^2$
Time, $t_1 = 0.5 \text{ min} = 30 \text{ s}$

Maximum velocity reached, $v = u + a_1 t_1 = 0 + 2.0 \times 30 = 60 \text{ m/s}$

Distance covered during acceleration, $s_1 = \dfrac{1}{2} a_1 t_1^2 = \dfrac{1}{2} \times 2.0 \times (30)^2 = 900 \text{ m}$

For the deceleration phase:
Initial velocity, $v = 60 \text{ m/s}$
Final velocity, $v_f = 0$
Time, $t_2 = 1 \text{ min} = 60 \text{ s}$

Distance covered during deceleration, $s_2 = \dfrac{v + v_f}{2} t_2 = \dfrac{60 + 0}{2} \times 60 = 1800 \text{ m}$

Total distance moved by the train, $s = s_1 + s_2 = 900 + 1800 = 2700 \text{ m}$

Converting to kilometers, $s = 2.7 \text{ km}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An object with a velocity of $4.0 \text{ m/s}$ is accelerated at a rate of $1.2 \text{ m/s}^2$ for $5.0 \text{ s}$. Determine the distance travelled during this period of acceleration in m.
questions.solution:
Using the second equation of motion:

$s = ut + \dfrac{1}{2}at^2$

Substituting the given values, $u = 4.0 \text{ m/s}$, $a = 1.2 \text{ m/s}^2$, and $t = 5.0 \text{ s}$:

$s = (4.0)(5.0) + \dfrac{1}{2}(1.2)(5.0)^2$

$s = 20.0 + (0.6)(25.0)$

$s = 20.0 + 15.0 = 35 \text{ m}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Starting from rest, a train travels with a constant acceleration of $2.0 \text{ m/s}^2$ for half a minute. Subsequently, the brakes are applied, bringing the train to rest in one minute. Determine the maximum speed attained by the train in m/s.
questions.solution:
The train starts from rest, so initial velocity $u = 0$.

The acceleration is $a = 2.0 \text{ m/s}^2$ for a time $t = 0.5 \text{ min} = 30 \text{ s}$.

The maximum speed is reached at the end of the acceleration phase.

Using the equation of kinematics $v = u + at$:

$v = 0 + 2.0 \times 30 = 60 \text{ m/s}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Starting from rest, a train travels with a constant acceleration of $2.0 \text{ m/s}^2$ for half a minute. Subsequently, the brakes are applied, bringing the train to rest in one minute. Determine the position(s) of the train at half the maximum speed.
questions.solution:
Maximum speed attained by the train is $v_{max} = u + a_1 t_1 = 0 + 2.0 \times 30 = 60 \text{ m/s}$.

Half of the maximum speed is $v = \dfrac{60}{2} = 30 \text{ m/s}$.

The train attains this speed twice: once during acceleration and once during deceleration.

During the acceleration phase, the position $s_1$ at $v = 30 \text{ m/s}$ is given by:

$v^2 = u^2 + 2a_1 s_1$

$30^2 = 0 + 2(2.0)s_1 \Rightarrow s_1 = \dfrac{900}{4} = 225 \text{ m}$

The total distance covered during the acceleration phase is:

$S_1 = \dfrac{1}{2} a_1 t_1^2 = \dfrac{1}{2}(2.0)(30)^2 = 900 \text{ m}$

During the deceleration phase, the train comes to rest from $60 \text{ m/s}$ in $60 \text{ s}$. The acceleration is:

$a_2 = \dfrac{0 - 60}{60} = -1.0 \text{ m/s}^2$

The distance $s_2$ covered during deceleration to reach $v = 30 \text{ m/s}$ is given by:

$v^2 = v_{max}^2 + 2a_2 s_2$

$30^2 = 60^2 + 2(-1.0)s_2 \Rightarrow 2s_2 = 3600 - 900 = 2700 \Rightarrow s_2 = 1350 \text{ m}$

The position of the train from the starting point during deceleration is:

$S_{total} = S_1 + s_2 = 900 + 1350 = 2250 \text{ m} = 2.25 \text{ km}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A particle starting from rest travels with constant acceleration. If it takes $5.0 \text{ s}$ to reach a speed of $18.0 \text{ km/h}$, determine the average velocity during this period in m/s.
questions.solution:
Initial velocity, $u = 0$

Final velocity, $v = 18.0 \text{ km/h} = 18.0 \times \dfrac{5}{18} \text{ m/s} = 5.0 \text{ m/s}$

For constant acceleration, the average velocity is given by:

$v_{\text{avg}} = \dfrac{u + v}{2}$

$v_{\text{avg}} = \dfrac{0 + 5.0}{2} = 2.5 \text{ m/s}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The figure presents the graph of velocity versus time for a particle going along the X-axis. Determine the acceleration in $\text{m/s}^2$.
questions.solution:
From the given velocity-time graph, the velocity at $t = 0$ is $u = 2 \text{ m/s}$.

The velocity at $t = 10 \text{ s}$ is $v = 8 \text{ m/s}$.

The acceleration of the particle is equal to the slope of the $v-t$ graph:

$a = \dfrac{\Delta v}{\Delta t} = \dfrac{8 - 2}{10 - 0}$

$a = \dfrac{6}{10} = 0.6 \text{ m/s}^2$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A bullet travelling with a velocity of $16 \text{ m/s}$ penetrates a tree trunk and comes to rest in $0.4 \text{ m}$. Determine the time taken during the retardation in seconds.
questions.solution:
Initial velocity, $u = 16 \text{ m/s}$

Final velocity, $v = 0 \text{ m/s}$

Distance covered, $s = 0.4 \text{ m}$

Using the equation of motion $s = \left(\dfrac{u + v}{2}\right)t$:

$0.4 = \left(\dfrac{16 + 0}{2}\right)t$

$0.4 = 8t$

$t = \dfrac{0.4}{8} = 0.05 \text{ s}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A bullet travelling at a speed of $350 \text{ m/s}$ enters a concrete wall and penetrates a distance of $5.0 \text{ cm}$ before coming to rest. Determine the deceleration in $\text{m/s}^2$.
questions.solution:
Using the third equation of motion, $v^2 = u^2 + 2as$

Given $u = 350 \text{ m/s}$, $v = 0 \text{ m/s}$, and $s = 5.0 \text{ cm} = 0.05 \text{ m}$

Substituting the values:

$0 = (350)^2 + 2a(0.05)$

$0 = 122500 + 0.1a$

$a = -1225000 \text{ m/s}^2$

The deceleration is the magnitude of negative acceleration, which is $1225000 \text{ m/s}^2$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A particle starting from rest travels with constant acceleration. If it takes $5.0 \text{ s}$ to reach a speed of $18.0 \text{ km/h}$, determine the distance travelled by the particle during this period in m.
questions.solution:
Initial velocity, $u = 0$

Final velocity, $v = 18.0 \text{ km/h} = 18.0 \times \dfrac{5}{18} \text{ m/s} = 5.0 \text{ m/s}$

Time taken, $t = 5.0 \text{ s}$

Using the first equation of motion, $v = u + at$

$5.0 = 0 + a(5.0)$

$a = 1.0 \text{ m/s}^2$

Using the second equation of motion, $s = ut + \dfrac{1}{2}at^2$

$s = 0 + \dfrac{1}{2}(1.0)(5.0)^2$

$s = \dfrac{25.0}{2} = 12.5 \text{ m}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A driver takes $0.20 \text{ s}$ to apply the brakes after seeing a need for it. This is termed the reaction time of the driver. If he is driving a car at a speed of $54 \text{ km/h}$ and the brakes cause a deceleration of $6.0 \text{ m/s}^2$, determine the distance travelled by the car after he sees the need to put the brakes on, in m.
questions.solution:
Initial speed of the car, $u = 54 \text{ km/h} = 54 \times \dfrac{5}{18} \text{ m/s} = 15 \text{ m/s}$

Reaction time of the driver, $t = 0.20 \text{ s}$

Distance travelled during the reaction time, $s_1 = u \times t = 15 \times 0.20 = 3.0 \text{ m}$

Deceleration of the car, $a = -6.0 \text{ m/s}^2$

Final velocity, $v = 0$

Using the equation of motion, $v^2 - u^2 = 2as_2$

$0 - (15)^2 = 2 \times (-6.0) \times s_2$

$s_2 = \dfrac{225}{12} = 18.75 \text{ m}$

Total distance travelled by the car, $s = s_1 + s_2 = 3.0 + 18.75 = 21.75 \text{ m}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
Determine the missing entries in the following table:
Car ModelDriver $X$
Reaction time $0.20 \text{ s}$		Driver $Y$
Reaction time $0.30 \text{ s}$
$A$ (deceleration on hard braking $= 6.0 \text{ m/s}^2$)Speed $= 54 \text{ km/h}$
Braking distance
$a = $ ............................
Total stopping distance
$b = $ ............................		Speed $= 72 \text{ km/h}$
Braking distance
$c = $ ............................
Total stopping distance
$d = $ ............................
$B$ (deceleration on hard braking $= 7.5 \text{ m/s}^2$)Speed $= 54 \text{ km/h}$
Braking distance
$e = $ ............................
Total stopping distance
$f = $ ............................		Speed $= 72 \text{ km/h}$
Braking distance
$g = $ ............................
Total stopping distance
$h = $ ............................
questions.solution:
The speeds are converted from $\text{km/h}$ to $\text{m/s}$:

$u_1 = 54 \text{ km/h} = 54 \times \dfrac{5}{18} = 15 \text{ m/s}$

$u_2 = 72 \text{ km/h} = 72 \times \dfrac{5}{18} = 20 \text{ m/s}$

The braking distance $S$ is given by $S = \dfrac{u^2}{2a}$ and the total stopping distance $D$ is given by $D = u t_r + S$, where $t_r$ is the reaction time.

For Car $A$ and Driver $X$ ($u = 15 \text{ m/s}$, $a = 6.0 \text{ m/s}^2$, $t_r = 0.20 \text{ s}$):
$a = \dfrac{15^2}{2 \times 6.0} = 18.75 \text{ m} \approx 19 \text{ m}$
$b = 15 \times 0.20 + 18.75 = 3.0 + 18.75 = 21.75 \text{ m} \approx 22 \text{ m}$

For Car $A$ and Driver $Y$ ($u = 20 \text{ m/s}$, $a = 6.0 \text{ m/s}^2$, $t_r = 0.30 \text{ s}$):
$c = \dfrac{20^2}{2 \times 6.0} = 33.33 \text{ m} \approx 33 \text{ m}$
$d = 20 \times 0.30 + 33.33 = 6.0 + 33.33 = 39.33 \text{ m} \approx 39 \text{ m}$

For Car $B$ and Driver $X$ ($u = 15 \text{ m/s}$, $a = 7.5 \text{ m/s}^2$, $t_r = 0.20 \text{ s}$):
$e = \dfrac{15^2}{2 \times 7.5} = 15 \text{ m}$
$f = 15 \times 0.20 + 15 = 3.0 + 15 = 18 \text{ m}$

For Car $B$ and Driver $Y$ ($u = 20 \text{ m/s}$, $a = 7.5 \text{ m/s}^2$, $t_r = 0.30 \text{ s}$):
$g = \dfrac{20^2}{2 \times 7.5} = 26.67 \text{ m} \approx 27 \text{ m}$
$h = 20 \times 0.30 + 26.67 = 6.0 + 26.67 = 32.67 \text{ m} \approx 33 \text{ m}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A police jeep chases a culprit on a motorbike. The motorbike crosses a turning at a speed of $72 \text{ km/h}$. The jeep follows at a speed of $90 \text{ km/h}$, crossing the turning ten seconds later than the bike. Assuming they travel at constant speeds, determine how far from the turning the jeep catches up with the bike, in km.
questions.solution:
Speed of the motorbike, $v_m = 72 \text{ km/h} = 72 \times \dfrac{5}{18} = 20 \text{ m/s}$

Speed of the police jeep, $v_j = 90 \text{ km/h} = 90 \times \dfrac{5}{18} = 25 \text{ m/s}$

The jeep crosses the turning $10 \text{ s}$ after the motorbike. In this time, the distance covered by the motorbike is $d_0 = 20 \times 10 = 200 \text{ m}$

Relative speed of the jeep with respect to the motorbike is $v_{rel} = 25 - 20 = 5 \text{ m/s}$

Time taken by the jeep to catch up with the motorbike is $t = \dfrac{200}{5} = 40 \text{ s}$

Distance from the turning where the jeep catches the motorbike is $d = v_j \times t = 25 \times 40 = 1000 \text{ m} = 1 \text{ km}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A ball is projected vertically upward at a speed of $50 \text{ m/s}$. Determine the maximum height in m. Take $g = 10 \text{ m/s}^2$.
questions.solution:
Using the equation of motion $v^2 = u^2 + 2as$.

At the maximum height, the final velocity $v = 0$.

Substituting the given values $u = 50 \text{ m/s}$ and $a = -g = -10 \text{ m/s}^2$:

$0 = (50)^2 - 2(10)H$

$20H = 2500$

$H = \dfrac{2500}{20} = 125 \text{ m}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A ball is projected vertically upward at a speed of $50 \text{ m/s}$. Determine the time required to reach the maximum height in s. Take $g = 10 \text{ m/s}^2$.
questions.solution:
Given initial velocity $u = 50 \text{ m/s}$ and acceleration due to gravity $g = 10 \text{ m/s}^2$.

At the maximum height, the final velocity of the ball becomes zero, so $v = 0$.

Using the first equation of motion $v = u - gt$:

$0 = 50 - 10t$

$10t = 50$

$t = 5 \text{ s}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A ball is projected vertically upward at a speed of $50 \text{ m/s}$. Determine the speed at half the maximum height. Take $g = 10 \text{ m/s}^2$.
questions.solution:
Initial speed $u = 50 \text{ m/s}$

Maximum height $H = \dfrac{u^2}{2g}$

Let $v$ be the speed at half the maximum height, $h = \dfrac{H}{2}$.

Using the equation of motion:

$v^2 = u^2 - 2gh$

$v^2 = u^2 - 2g \left(\dfrac{H}{2}\right)$

$v^2 = u^2 - gH$

Substituting $H = \dfrac{u^2}{2g}$:

$v^2 = u^2 - g \left(\dfrac{u^2}{2g}\right) = u^2 - \dfrac{u^2}{2} = \dfrac{u^2}{2}$

$v = \dfrac{u}{\sqrt{2}}$

$v = \dfrac{50}{\sqrt{2}} = 25\sqrt{2} \text{ m/s}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A ball is dropped from a balloon ascending at a speed of $7 \text{ m/s}$. If the balloon was at a height of $60 \text{ m}$ at the time the ball was dropped, determine the time taken by the ball to reach the ground in seconds.
questions.solution:
Taking the upward direction as positive.

Initial velocity of the ball, $u = 7 \text{ m/s}$

Displacement of the ball, $s = -60 \text{ m}$

Acceleration due to gravity, $a = -9.8 \text{ m/s}^2$

Using the equation of motion $s = ut + \dfrac{1}{2}at^2$:

$-60 = 7t - \dfrac{1}{2}(9.8)t^2$

$-60 = 7t - 4.9t^2$

$4.9t^2 - 7t - 60 = 0$

Multiplying by $10$ on both sides:

$49t^2 - 70t - 600 = 0$

$49t^2 - 210t + 140t - 600 = 0$

$7t(7t - 30) + 20(7t - 30) = 0$

$(7t + 20)(7t - 30) = 0$

Since time cannot be negative, we get $7t - 30 = 0 \Rightarrow t = \dfrac{30}{7} \text{ s}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Travelling at $43.2 \text{ km/h}$, a person applies the brake to his scooter, producing a deceleration of $6.0 \text{ m/s}^2$. Determine how far it will travel before stopping, in meters.
questions.solution:
Initial velocity, $u = 43.2 \text{ km/h} = 43.2 \times \dfrac{5}{18} \text{ m/s} = 12 \text{ m/s}$

Final velocity, $v = 0 \text{ m/s}$

Acceleration, $a = -6.0 \text{ m/s}^2$

Using the third equation of motion, $v^2 - u^2 = 2as$

$0^2 - (12)^2 = 2(-6.0)s$

$-144 = -12s$

$s = 12 \text{ m}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The $x$-$t$ graph of a particle is displayed in the figure. Determine the time $t$, in seconds, such that the average velocity of the particle during the period $0$ to $t$ is zero.
questions.solution:
The average velocity of a particle is given by the total displacement divided by the total time taken.

$v_{avg} = \dfrac{x(t) - x(0)}{t - 0}$

For the average velocity to be zero, the displacement must be zero, which implies $x(t) = x(0)$.

From the given $x$-$t$ graph, the initial position of the particle at $t = 0$ is $x(0) = 20$ m.

The particle must return to the position $x = 20$ m for the displacement to be zero.

Observing the graph, the horizontal line at $x = 20$ m intersects the curve again at $t = 12$ s.

Therefore, the time $t$ such that the average velocity is zero is $12$ s.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A person sitting on the top of a tall building is dropping balls at regular intervals of one second. Determine the positions of the 3rd, 4th and 5th ball when the 6th ball is being dropped.
options: $45 \text{ m}$, $20 \text{ m}$ and $5 \text{ m}$ below the top
$78.4 \text{ m}$, $44.1 \text{ m}$ and $19.6 \text{ m}$ below the top
$19.6 \text{ m}$, $4.9 \text{ m}$ and $0 \text{ m}$ below the top
$44.1 \text{ m}$, $19.6 \text{ m}$ and $4.9 \text{ m}$ below the top
questions.solution:
Let the time when the 6th ball is dropped be $t = 0$.

Since the balls are dropped at regular intervals of $1 \text{ s}$, the time elapsed for the other balls are:
Time for 5th ball, $t_5 = 1 \text{ s}$
Time for 4th ball, $t_4 = 2 \text{ s}$
Time for 3rd ball, $t_3 = 3 \text{ s}$

The distance fallen by a freely falling body in time $t$ is given by $h = \dfrac{1}{2}gt^2$. Taking $g = 9.8 \text{ m/s}^2$:

Distance of the 3rd ball from the top:
$h_3 = \dfrac{1}{2} \times 9.8 \times (3)^2 = 4.9 \times 9 = 44.1 \text{ m}$

Distance of the 4th ball from the top:
$h_4 = \dfrac{1}{2} \times 9.8 \times (2)^2 = 4.9 \times 4 = 19.6 \text{ m}$

Distance of the 5th ball from the top:
$h_5 = \dfrac{1}{2} \times 9.8 \times (1)^2 = 4.9 \times 1 = 4.9 \text{ m}$

The positions of the 3rd, 4th, and 5th balls are $44.1 \text{ m}$, $19.6 \text{ m}$, and $4.9 \text{ m}$ below the top respectively.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Standing at a distance of $7 \text{ m}$ from an $11.8 \text{ m}$ high building, a healthy young man sees a kid slipping from the top floor. Determine the speed (assumed uniform) with which he should run to catch the kid at arms height ($1.8 \text{ m}$) in m/s.
questions.solution:
The distance fallen by the kid before being caught is:

$h = 11.8 - 1.8 = 10 \text{ m}$

Using the second equation of motion for the freely falling kid ($u = 0$):

$h = ut + \dfrac{1}{2}gt^2$

$10 = 0 + \dfrac{1}{2} \times 9.8 \times t^2$

$10 = 4.9 t^2$

$t^2 = \dfrac{10}{4.9} = \dfrac{100}{49}$

$t = \dfrac{10}{7} \text{ s}$

The man must cover a horizontal distance of $7 \text{ m}$ in this time $t$ to catch the kid. Since his speed is uniform, the required speed $v$ is:

$v = \dfrac{\text{Distance}}{\text{Time}} = \dfrac{7}{\dfrac{10}{7}}$

$v = \dfrac{49}{10} = 4.9 \text{ m/s}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A particle starts from point $A$ and travels along the solid curve shown in the figure. Determine approximately the position $B$ of the particle such that the average velocity between positions $A$ and $B$ shares the same direction as the instantaneous velocity at $B$.
questions.solution:
The direction of average velocity between points $A$ and $B$ is along the secant line connecting $A$ and $B$.

The direction of instantaneous velocity at point $B$ is along the tangent to the curve at $B$.

For these two velocities to have the same direction, the secant line passing through $A$ and $B$ must be tangent to the curve at point $B$.

From the given graph, the coordinates of the starting point $A$ are approximately $(1 \text{ m}, 4 \text{ m})$.

We can evaluate the given options by checking if the point lies on the curve and if the secant line is tangent to the curve at that point:

For option (1) $B(3 \text{ m}, 5 \text{ m})$: This point does not lie on the curve shown in the graph.

For option (2) $B(4 \text{ m}, 2 \text{ m})$: The slope of the secant line is $m = \dfrac{2 - 4}{4 - 1} = -\dfrac{2}{3}$. However, at $x = 4 \text{ m}$, the curve is rising, so the tangent has a positive slope. This does not match.

For option (4) $B(2 \text{ m}, 1 \text{ m})$: The slope of the secant line is $m = \dfrac{1 - 4}{2 - 1} = -3$. The curve is concave up in this region, meaning any tangent line will lie below the curve and cannot pass through point $A$.

For option (3) $B(5 \text{ m}, 3 \text{ m})$: The slope of the secant line is $m = \dfrac{3 - 4}{5 - 1} = -\dfrac{1}{4} = -0.25$. Looking at the graph, the point $x = 5 \text{ m}$ is slightly past the local maximum, where the curve is sloping downwards. A tangent line with a gentle negative slope of $-0.25$ perfectly matches the visual representation and passes through $A(1, 4)$.

Thus, the correct position is $x = 5 \text{ m}, y = 3 \text{ m}$.

Answer: $x = 5 \text{ m}, y = 3 \text{ m}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A ball is dropped onto a sandy floor from a height of $5 \text{ m}$ and penetrates the sand up to $10 \text{ cm}$ before coming to rest. Assuming the retardation of the ball in the sand is uniform, determine its value in $\text{m/s}^2$.
questions.solution:
The velocity of the ball just before entering the sand is given by the equation of motion:

$v^2 = u^2 + 2gh$

Since the ball is dropped, its initial velocity $u = 0$. Taking $g = 9.8 \text{ m/s}^2$ and $h = 5 \text{ m}$:

$v^2 = 0 + 2 \times 9.8 \times 5 = 98 \text{ m}^2/\text{s}^2$

Let $a$ be the uniform retardation of the ball inside the sand. The final velocity $v_f$ becomes zero after penetrating a distance $s = 10 \text{ cm} = 0.1 \text{ m}$.

Using the third equation of motion for the motion inside the sand:

$v_f^2 = v^2 - 2as$

Substituting the values:

$0 = 98 - 2 \times a \times 0.1$

$0.2a = 98$

$a = \dfrac{98}{0.2} = 490 \text{ m/s}^2$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An elevator is descending with uniform acceleration. To determine the acceleration, a person in the elevator drops a coin at the instant the elevator starts. The coin is $6 \text{ ft}$ above the elevator floor at the time it is dropped. The person observes that the coin strikes the floor in $1$ second. From these data, calculate the acceleration of the elevator in $\text{ft/s}^2$.
questions.solution:
Let the downward acceleration of the elevator be $a$.

The acceleration of the freely falling coin is $g = 32 \text{ ft/s}^2$ downwards.

The relative acceleration of the coin with respect to the elevator is $a_{rel} = g - a$ downwards.

Since the coin is dropped at the instant the elevator starts, the initial relative velocity of the coin is $u_{rel} = 0$.

The relative displacement of the coin is $s_{rel} = 6 \text{ ft}$ and the time taken is $t = 1 \text{ s}$.

Using the equation of kinematics for relative motion:

$s_{rel} = u_{rel}t + \dfrac{1}{2}a_{rel}t^2$

Substituting the values:

$6 = 0 + \dfrac{1}{2}(32 - a)(1)^2$

$12 = 32 - a$

$a = 20 \text{ ft/s}^2$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: From a point $100 \text{ m}$ above the ground, a ball is thrown horizontally with a speed of $20 \text{ m/s}$. Determine the horizontal distance the ball travels before reaching the ground in meters.
questions.solution:
Let the time taken by the ball to reach the ground be $t$.

Using the second equation of motion in the vertical direction:

$h = u_y t + \dfrac{1}{2} g t^2$

Since the ball is thrown horizontally, the initial vertical velocity $u_y = 0$. Assuming $g = 10 \text{ m/s}^2$:

$100 = 0 + \dfrac{1}{2} (10) t^2$

$100 = 5t^2$

$t^2 = 20 \Rightarrow t = \sqrt{20} = 2\sqrt{5} \text{ s}$

The horizontal distance (range) traveled by the ball is given by:

$R = u_x \times t$

$R = 20 \times 2\sqrt{5} = 40\sqrt{5} \text{ m}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: From a point $100 \text{ m}$ above the ground, a ball is thrown horizontally with a speed of $20 \text{ m/s}$. Determine the velocity (both magnitude and direction) with which it strikes the ground.
questions.solution:
Initial horizontal velocity, $u_x = 20 \text{ m/s}$

Initial vertical velocity, $u_y = 0$

Vertical distance, $h = 100 \text{ m}$

Using the equation of motion for the vertical direction:

$v_y^2 = u_y^2 + 2gh$

$v_y^2 = 0 + 2 \times 9.8 \times 100 = 1960$

$v_y = \sqrt{1960} \approx 44.27 \text{ m/s}$

The horizontal velocity remains constant throughout the motion, $v_x = 20 \text{ m/s}$

Magnitude of the final velocity is given by:

$v = \sqrt{v_x^2 + v_y^2} = \sqrt{20^2 + 1960} = \sqrt{400 + 1960} = \sqrt{2360} \approx 48.58 \text{ m/s} \approx 49 \text{ m/s}$

Let $\theta$ be the angle the velocity vector makes with the horizontal:

$\tan \theta = \dfrac{v_y}{v_x} = \dfrac{44.27}{20} \approx 2.21$

$\theta = \tan^{-1}(2.21) \approx 66^\circ$

Thus, the ball strikes the ground with a velocity of $49 \text{ m/s}$ at an angle of $66^\circ$ with the horizontal.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A ball is projected at a speed of $40 \text{ m/s}$ at an angle of $60^\circ$ to the horizontal. Determine the maximum height reached by the ball in m. Take $g = 10 \text{ m/s}^2$.
questions.solution:
The maximum height $H$ reached by a projectile is given by the formula:

$H = \dfrac{u^2 \sin^2 \theta}{2g}$

Given $u = 40 \text{ m/s}$, $\theta = 60^\circ$, and $g = 10 \text{ m/s}^2$.

Substituting the values:

$H = \dfrac{(40)^2 (\sin 60^\circ)^2}{2 \times 10}$

$H = \dfrac{1600 \times \left(\dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right)^2}{20}$

$H = \dfrac{1600 \times \dfrac{3}{4}}{20}$

$H = \dfrac{1200}{20} = 60 \text{ m}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A ball is projected at a speed of $40 \text{ m/s}$ at an angle of $60^\circ$ to the horizontal. Determine the range of the ball. Take $g = 10 \text{ m/s}^2$.
questions.solution:
The horizontal range of a projectile is given by the formula:

$R = \dfrac{u^2 \sin(2\theta)}{g}$

Substituting the given values $u = 40 \text{ m/s}$, $\theta = 60^\circ$, and $g = 10 \text{ m/s}^2$:

$R = \dfrac{(40)^2 \sin(2 \times 60^\circ)}{10}$

$R = \dfrac{1600 \sin(120^\circ)}{10}$

Since $\sin(120^\circ) = \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$:

$R = 160 \times \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} = 80\sqrt{3} \text{ m}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Travelling at $60 \text{ km/h}$, a car overtakes another car travelling at $42 \text{ km/h}$. Assuming each car is $5.0 \text{ m}$ long, determine the time taken during the overtake and the total road distance used for the overtake.
questions.solution:
The speeds of the two cars in $\text{m/s}$ are:
$v_A = 60 \times \dfrac{5}{18} = \dfrac{50}{3} \text{ m/s}$
$v_B = 42 \times \dfrac{5}{18} = \dfrac{35}{3} \text{ m/s}$

The relative speed of car A with respect to car B is:
$v_{rel} = v_A - v_B = \dfrac{50}{3} - \dfrac{35}{3} = 5 \text{ m/s}$

For car A to completely overtake car B, the front of car A must travel from the rear of car B to a point where the rear of car A is just ahead of the front of car B. The total relative distance to be covered is the sum of the lengths of both cars:
$d_{rel} = L_A + L_B = 5.0 + 5.0 = 10.0 \text{ m}$

The time taken for the overtake is:
$t = \dfrac{d_{rel}}{v_{rel}} = \dfrac{10.0}{5} = 2 \text{ s}$

During this time, the actual distance travelled by car A is:
$d_A = v_A \times t = \dfrac{50}{3} \times 2 = \dfrac{100}{3} \approx 33.33 \text{ m}$

The total road distance used for the overtake is the total length of the road occupied during the maneuver, which is measured from the initial position of the rear of car A to the final position of the front of car A. This is equivalent to the distance travelled by car A plus its own length:
$\text{Total road distance} = d_A + L_A = 33.33 + 5.0 = 38.33 \text{ m}$

Rounding to the nearest integer, the total road distance used is $38 \text{ m}$.

Answer: $2 \text{ s}$, $38 \text{ m}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Goli is a popular game in Indian villages, played with small glass balls known as golis. One player's goli is located at a distance of $2.0 \text{ m}$ from the second player's goli. The second player must project his goli by keeping the thumb of the left hand at the position of his goli, holding the goli between his two middle fingers, and executing the throw. The second player wins if the projected goli strikes the first player's goli. Assuming the goli is projected horizontally from a height of $19.6 \text{ cm}$ above the ground, determine the speed in m/s with which it must be projected to directly hit the stationary goli without falling on the ground earlier. (Take $g = 9.8 \text{ m/s}^2$)
questions.solution:
The vertical motion of the goli is given by $h = \dfrac{1}{2} g t^2$.

Substituting the given values, $0.196 = \dfrac{1}{2} \times 9.8 \times t^2$.

$t^2 = \dfrac{0.196}{4.9} = 0.04 \Rightarrow t = 0.2 \text{ s}$.

The horizontal motion is uniform, so the range is $R = u t$.

Substituting $R = 2.0 \text{ m}$ and $t = 0.2 \text{ s}$, we get $2.0 = u \times 0.2$.

$u = \dfrac{2.0}{0.2} = 10 \text{ m/s}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A stone is thrown vertically upward with a speed of $28 \text{ m/s}$. Determine the maximum height reached by the stone, its velocity one second before it reaches the maximum height, and whether this velocity changes if the initial speed is more than $28 \text{ m/s}$, such as $40 \text{ m/s}$ or $80 \text{ m/s}$.
questions.solution:
Using the third equation of motion, $v^2 = u^2 - 2gH$.

At maximum height, $v = 0$. Thus, $H = \dfrac{u^2}{2g}$.

Substituting $u = 28 \text{ m/s}$ and $g = 9.8 \text{ m/s}^2$:

$H = \dfrac{28^2}{2 \times 9.8} = \dfrac{784}{19.6} = 40 \text{ m}$.

Let $T$ be the time taken to reach maximum height. At maximum height, $v = 0$, so $0 = u - gT \Rightarrow T = \dfrac{u}{g}$.

The velocity one second before reaching maximum height is at time $t = T - 1$.

Using $v' = u - gt$:

$v' = u - g(T - 1) = u - g\left(\dfrac{u}{g} - 1\right) = u - u + g = g = 9.8 \text{ m/s}$.

Since $v'$ depends only on $g$ and is independent of the initial velocity $u$, this velocity will not change if the initial speed is $40 \text{ m/s}$ or $80 \text{ m/s}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A person standing on the top of a cliff $171 \text{ ft}$ high needs to throw a packet to his friend standing on the ground $228 \text{ ft}$ horizontally away. If he throws the packet directly aiming at the friend with a speed of $15.0 \text{ ft/s}$, how short will the packet fall in ft?
questions.solution:
Let the point of projection be the origin $(0,0)$. The friend is on the ground at a horizontal distance of $228 \text{ ft}$ and a vertical depth of $171 \text{ ft}$, so their coordinates are $(228, -171)$.

Since the packet is aimed directly at the friend, the angle of projection $\theta$ below the horizontal is given by:

$\tan \theta = \dfrac{171}{228} = \dfrac{3}{4}$

This gives $\sin \theta = \dfrac{3}{5}$ and $\cos \theta = \dfrac{4}{5}$.

The initial speed of the packet is $u = 15.0 \text{ ft/s}$. The horizontal and vertical components of the initial velocity are:

$u_x = u \cos \theta = 15 \times \dfrac{4}{5} = 12 \text{ ft/s}$

$u_y = -u \sin \theta = -15 \times \dfrac{3}{5} = -9 \text{ ft/s}$

Taking the acceleration due to gravity as $g = 32 \text{ ft/s}^2$, the vertical displacement $S_y$ is $-171 \text{ ft}$. Using the second equation of motion:

$S_y = u_y t - \dfrac{1}{2} g t^2$

$-171 = -9t - \dfrac{1}{2}(32)t^2$

$-171 = -9t - 16t^2$

$16t^2 + 9t - 171 = 0$

Solving the quadratic equation for $t$:

$t = \dfrac{-9 \pm \sqrt{81 - 4(16)(-171)}}{2(16)}$

$t = \dfrac{-9 \pm \sqrt{81 + 10944}}{32} = \dfrac{-9 \pm \sqrt{11025}}{32}$

$t = \dfrac{-9 \pm 105}{32}$

Taking the positive root, the time of flight is:

$t = \dfrac{96}{32} = 3 \text{ s}$

The horizontal distance traveled by the packet in this time is:

$x = u_x t = 12 \times 3 = 36 \text{ ft}$

The distance by which the packet falls short of the friend is:

$\text{Distance short} = 228 - 36 = 192 \text{ ft}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A ball is projected from a point on the floor at a speed of $15 \text{ m/s}$ and an angle of $60^\circ$ with the horizontal. Decide whether it will hit a vertical wall $5 \text{ m}$ away from the point of projection and perpendicular to the plane of projection without hitting the floor. Also determine if the answer will differ if the wall is $22 \text{ m}$ away.
options: No, it will not hit the $5 \text{ m}$ wall; No, the answer does not differ for the $22 \text{ m}$ wall.
No, it will not hit the $5 \text{ m}$ wall; Yes, the answer differs for the $22 \text{ m}$ wall.
Yes, it will hit the $5 \text{ m}$ wall; Yes, the answer differs for the $22 \text{ m}$ wall.
Yes, it will hit the $5 \text{ m}$ wall; No, the answer does not differ for the $22 \text{ m}$ wall.
questions.solution:
The horizontal range of the projectile is given by $R = \dfrac{u^2 \sin 2\theta}{g}$.

Substituting the given values $u = 15 \text{ m/s}$, $\theta = 60^\circ$, and $g = 9.8 \text{ m/s}^2$:

$R = \dfrac{(15)^2 \sin(120^\circ)}{9.8} = \dfrac{225 \times \sqrt{3}}{2 \times 9.8} \approx 19.88 \text{ m}$

Since the distance to the first wall is $5 \text{ m}$, which is less than the range ($5 \text{ m} < 19.88 \text{ m}$), the ball will hit the wall before hitting the floor. Thus, the answer for the $5 \text{ m}$ wall is Yes.

For the second wall at $22 \text{ m}$, the distance is greater than the range ($22 \text{ m} > 19.88 \text{ m}$). The ball will hit the floor before reaching this wall. Thus, it will not hit the $22 \text{ m}$ wall without hitting the floor first.

Therefore, it will hit the $5 \text{ m}$ wall, and the answer differs for the $22 \text{ m}$ wall.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Determine the average velocity of a projectile between the instants it crosses half the maximum height. It is projected with a speed $u$ at an angle $	heta$ with the horizontal.
options: $u \sin\theta$, horizontal in the plane of projection
$u \cos\theta$, vertical in the plane of projection
$\dfrac{u \cos\theta}{2}$, horizontal in the plane of projection
$u \cos\theta$, horizontal in the plane of projection
questions.solution:
The average velocity is defined as the ratio of total displacement to total time taken.

Let the two instants when the projectile crosses half the maximum height be $t_1$ and $t_2$.

Since the vertical positions at these two instants are equal, the vertical displacement is zero.

The horizontal velocity of the projectile remains constant throughout the motion and is equal to $u \cos\theta$.

The horizontal displacement between the two instants is $\Delta x = u \cos\theta (t_2 - t_1)$.

The average velocity is $v_{avg} = \dfrac{\Delta x}{\Delta t} = \dfrac{u \cos\theta (t_2 - t_1)}{t_2 - t_1} = u \cos\theta$.

The direction of the average velocity is horizontal in the plane of projection.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A bomb is dropped from a plane travelling horizontally with uniform speed. Determine the location of the bomb relative to the plane at the time of explosion, and state whether this conclusion is true if the plane travels with uniform speed but not horizontally.
options: Behind the plane; the conclusion is false.
Behind the plane; the conclusion is true.
Vertically below the plane; the conclusion is true.
Vertically below the plane; the conclusion is false.
questions.solution:
Let the velocity of the plane be $\vec{v} = v_x \hat{i} + v_y \hat{j}$. Since the plane moves with uniform velocity, its horizontal and vertical displacements at any time $t$ are $x_p = v_x t$ and $y_p = v_y t$.

When the bomb is dropped, it inherits the initial velocity of the plane. The horizontal velocity of the bomb remains constant because there is no horizontal acceleration (neglecting air resistance). Its horizontal displacement at time $t$ is $x_b = v_x t$.

The vertical displacement of the bomb under gravity is $y_b = v_y t - \dfrac{1}{2} g t^2$.

Since $x_p = x_b$ at all times, the bomb will always be exactly vertically below the plane.

This result depends only on the horizontal component of the velocity being constant and equal for both the plane and the bomb. It is independent of the vertical component of the plane's velocity $v_y$. Therefore, the conclusion remains true even if the plane travels with uniform speed in a non-horizontal straight line.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A ball is dropped from a height. If it requires $0.200 \text{ s}$ to cross the final $6.00 \text{ m}$ before striking the ground, determine the height, in m, from which it was dropped. Take $g = 10 \text{ m/s}^2$.
questions.solution:
Let the velocity of the ball at the beginning of the last $6.00 \text{ m}$ be $u$.

Using the equation of motion for the last $6.00 \text{ m}$:

$s = ut + \dfrac{1}{2}gt^2$

$6.00 = u(0.200) + \dfrac{1}{2}(10)(0.200)^2$

$6.00 = 0.200u + 5(0.04)$

$6.00 = 0.200u + 0.20$

$0.200u = 5.80$

$u = 29 \text{ m/s}$

This velocity is acquired by the ball after falling a height $h_1$ from rest.

Using $v^2 = u^2 + 2gh_1$ where initial velocity is zero:

$(29)^2 = 0 + 2(10)h_1$

$841 = 20h_1$

$h_1 = 42.05 \text{ m}$

The total height from which the ball was dropped is $H = h_1 + 6.00$.

$H = 42.05 + 6.00 = 48.05 \text{ m}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An NCC parade is travelling at a uniform speed of $6 \text{ km/h}$ through a place under a berry tree, on which a bird is sitting at a height of $12.1 \text{ m}$. At a specific instant, the bird drops a berry. Determine the distance from the tree at that instant, in m, of the cadet who will receive the berry on his uniform.
questions.solution:
Speed of the cadet, $v = 6 \text{ km/h} = 6 \times \dfrac{5}{18} \text{ m/s} = \dfrac{5}{3} \text{ m/s}$

Height of the bird, $h = 12.1 \text{ m}$

The initial velocity of the dropped berry is $u = 0$.

Using the equation of kinematics for the vertical motion of the berry:

$h = ut + \dfrac{1}{2}gt^2$

$12.1 = 0 + \dfrac{1}{2}(9.8)t^2$

$12.1 = 4.9t^2$

$t^2 = \dfrac{12.1}{4.9} = \dfrac{121}{49}$

$t = \dfrac{11}{7} \text{ s}$

The distance of the cadet from the tree at the instant the berry was dropped is equal to the distance travelled by the cadet during the time $t$.

$d = v \times t = \dfrac{5}{3} \times \dfrac{11}{7} = \dfrac{55}{21} \text{ m}$

$d \approx 2.62 \text{ m}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A staircase contains three steps, each $10\text{ cm}$ high and $20 \text{ cm}$ wide, as shown in the figure. Determine the minimum horizontal velocity, in m/s, of a ball rolling off the uppermost plane so that it directly hits the lowest plane.
questions.solution:
Let the edge of the uppermost plane be the origin $(0,0)$.

The staircase has steps of height $h = 10 \text{ cm} = 0.1 \text{ m}$ and width $w = 20 \text{ cm} = 0.2 \text{ m}$.

From the given figure, to directly hit the lowest plane, the ball must clear the edge of the second step.

The coordinates of the edge of the second step are $x = 2w = 0.4 \text{ m}$ and $y = -2h = -0.2 \text{ m}$.

The equation of the trajectory of the ball is given by:

$y = -\dfrac{gx^2}{2u^2}$

For the ball to just clear the second step, its trajectory must pass through the point $(0.4, -0.2)$. Substituting these values into the trajectory equation:

$-0.2 = -\dfrac{10 \times (0.4)^2}{2u^2}$

$0.2 = \dfrac{10 \times 0.16}{2u^2}$

$0.2 = \dfrac{0.8}{u^2}$

$u^2 = \dfrac{0.8}{0.2} = 4$

$u = 2 \text{ m/s}$

Thus, the minimum horizontal velocity required is $2 \text{ m/s}$.

Answer: $2$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: From a point $100 \text{ m}$ above the ground, a ball is thrown horizontally with a speed of $20 \text{ m/s}$. Determine the time it takes to reach the ground in seconds.
questions.solution:
The initial vertical velocity of the ball is $u_y = 0$.

The vertical displacement is $s_y = 100 \text{ m}$.

Using the second equation of motion for the vertical direction:

$s_y = u_y t + \dfrac{1}{2} g t^2$

Substituting the given values and taking $g = 9.8 \text{ m/s}^2$:

$100 = 0 + \dfrac{1}{2} \times 9.8 \times t^2$

$100 = 4.9 t^2$

$t^2 = \dfrac{100}{4.9} = \dfrac{1000}{49}$

$t = \dfrac{10\sqrt{10}}{7} \approx 4.5 \text{ s}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A person is standing on a truck moving with a constant velocity of $14.7 \text{ m/s}$ on a horizontal road. The man throws a ball in such a way that it returns to the truck after the truck has travelled $58.8 \text{ m}$. Determine the speed and the angle of projection as seen from the road.
questions.solution:
The velocity of the truck is $v_t = 14.7 \text{ m/s}$.

The time taken by the truck to travel $58.8 \text{ m}$ is the time of flight of the ball.

$T = \dfrac{58.8}{14.7} = 4 \text{ s}$

Since the ball returns to the truck, its horizontal velocity as seen from the road must be equal to the velocity of the truck.

$u_x = v_t = 14.7 \text{ m/s}$

The time of flight is given by $T = \dfrac{2u_y}{g}$.

$4 = \dfrac{2u_y}{9.8} \Rightarrow u_y = 19.6 \text{ m/s}$

The speed of projection as seen from the road is $u = \sqrt{u_x^2 + u_y^2}$.

$u = \sqrt{(14.7)^2 + (19.6)^2} = \sqrt{216.09 + 384.16} = \sqrt{600.25} = 24.5 \text{ m/s}$

The angle of projection $\theta$ with the horizontal is given by $\tan \theta = \dfrac{u_y}{u_x}$.

$\tan \theta = \dfrac{19.6}{14.7} = \dfrac{4}{3}$

$\theta = 53^\circ$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A person is standing on a truck moving with a constant velocity of $14.7 \text{ m/s}$ on a horizontal road. The man throws a ball in such a way that it returns to the truck after the truck has travelled $58.8 \text{ m}$. Determine the speed and the angle of projection as seen from the truck.
questions.solution:
The time taken by the truck to travel $58.8 \text{ m}$ is given by:

$t = \dfrac{d}{v} = \dfrac{58.8}{14.7} = 4 \text{ s}$

Since the ball returns to the moving truck, its horizontal displacement relative to the truck is zero. This implies that the horizontal component of the ball's velocity relative to the truck is zero. Thus, the ball is thrown vertically upwards as seen from the truck.

The time of flight of the ball is equal to the time taken by the truck, so $T = 4 \text{ s}$.

Using the formula for the time of flight of a projectile thrown vertically upwards:

$T = \dfrac{2u}{g}$

$4 = \dfrac{2u}{9.8}$

$u = \dfrac{4 \times 9.8}{2} = 19.6 \text{ m/s}$

Therefore, the speed of projection as seen from the truck is $19.6 \text{ m/s}$ upward.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The benches of a gallery in a cricket stadium are $1 \text{ m}$ wide and $1 \text{ m}$ high. A batsman strikes the ball at a level one metre above the ground and hits a mammoth sixer. The ball starts at $35 \text{ m/s}$ at an angle of $53^\circ$ with the horizontal. The benches are perpendicular to the plane of motion and the first bench is $110 \text{ m}$ from the batsman. Determine the number of the bench on which the ball will land. (Provide the bench number as an integer).
questions.solution:
The total horizontal distance the bike must cover to safely cross the ditch is the sum of the width of the ditch and the horizontal length of the bike.

Horizontal length of the bike $= L \cos \theta = 5 \cos 15^\circ \approx 5 \times 0.966 = 4.83$ ft.

Total required horizontal range $R = 11.7 + 4.83 = 16.53$ ft.

Using the formula for the horizontal range of a projectile, $R = \dfrac{v^2 \sin(2\theta)}{g}$.

Substituting $R = 16.53$ ft, $\theta = 15^\circ$, and standard gravity in US customary units $g = 32$ ft/s$^2$:

$16.53 = \dfrac{v^2 \sin(30^\circ)}{32}$

$16.53 = \dfrac{v^2 \times 0.5}{32}$

$v^2 = \dfrac{16.53 \times 32}{0.5} = 1057.92$

$v = \sqrt{1057.92} \approx 32.5$ ft/s.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: As shown in the figure, a ditch is $11.7 \text{ ft}$ wide and has approach roads inclined at an angle of $15^\circ$ with the horizontal. Determine the minimum speed in ft/s at which a motorbike must travel on the road to safely cross the ditch. Assume the bike has a length of $5 \text{ ft}$ and that it leaves the road exactly when its front part runs out of the approach road.
questions.solution:
The bike acts as a projectile starting the moment its front wheel reaches the edge.

At this instant, the bike is on the incline, so the horizontal distance of its back wheel from the edge is $5 \\cos 15^\\circ$.

To safely cross, the back of the bike must clear the ditch. Thus, the total required horizontal range is:

$R = 11.7 + 5 \\cos 15^\\circ$

$R \\approx 11.7 + 5(0.966) = 11.7 + 4.83 = 16.53 \\text{ ft}$

Using the horizontal range formula $R = \\dfrac{u^2 \\sin 2\\theta}{g}$ with $g = 32 \\text{ ft/s}^2$ and $\\theta = 15^\\circ$:

$16.53 = \\dfrac{u^2 \\sin 30^\\circ}{32}$

$16.53 = \\dfrac{u^2 (0.5)}{32}$

$u^2 = \\dfrac{16.53 \\times 32}{0.5} = 1057.92$

$u = \\sqrt{1057.92} \\approx 32.5 \\text{ ft/s}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A river $400 \text{ m}$ wide flows at a rate of $2.0 \text{ m/s}$. A boat sails at a velocity of $10 \text{ m/s}$ with respect to the water, in a direction perpendicular to the river. Determine the time taken by the boat to reach the opposite bank, in seconds.
questions.solution:
The width of the river is $d = 400 \text{ m}$.

The velocity of the boat with respect to the water is $v_{br} = 10 \text{ m/s}$.

Since the boat sails in a direction perpendicular to the river flow, the component of its velocity along the width of the river is exactly $v_{br}$.

The time taken to cross the river is given by the ratio of the width of the river to the component of the boat's velocity perpendicular to the river flow.

$t = \dfrac{d}{v_{br}}$

$t = \dfrac{400}{10} = 40 \text{ s}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Sitting on the shore of a river, a man is in the line of a $1.0 \text{ m}$ long boat and is $5.5 \text{ m}$ away from the centre of the boat. He wishes to throw an apple into the boat. If he is able to throw the apple only with a speed of $10 \text{ m/s}$, determine the minimum and maximum angles of projection for a successful shot. Assume that the point of projection and the edge of the boat are in the same horizontal level. (Take $g = 10 \text{ m/s}^2$)
options: Minimum angle $18.5^\circ$, maximum angle $71.5^\circ$ with a forbidden interval of $53^\circ$ between them
Minimum angle $15^\circ$, maximum angle $75^\circ$ with a forbidden interval of $45^\circ$ between them
Minimum angle $15^\circ$, maximum angle $75^\circ$ with a forbidden interval of $53^\circ$ between them
Minimum angle $30^\circ$, maximum angle $60^\circ$ with a forbidden interval of $15^\circ$ between them
questions.solution:
The distance of the centre of the boat from the man is $5.5 \text{ m}$ and its length is $1.0 \text{ m}$.

The nearest and farthest edges of the boat are at distances $5.5 - 0.5 = 5.0 \text{ m}$ and $5.5 + 0.5 = 6.0 \text{ m}$ respectively.

For a successful shot, the range $R$ of the projectile must satisfy $5.0 \le R \le 6.0$.

The range of a projectile is given by $R = \dfrac{v^2 \sin 2\theta}{g}$.

Substituting $v = 10 \text{ m/s}$ and $g = 10 \text{ m/s}^2$, we get:

$R = \dfrac{10^2 \sin 2\theta}{10} = 10 \sin 2\theta$

Therefore, the inequality becomes:

$5.0 \le 10 \sin 2\theta \le 6.0$

$0.5 \le \sin 2\theta \le 0.6$

The principal values for $2\theta$ are between $30^\circ$ and $37^\circ$. Since $\sin(180^\circ - x) = \sin x$, the other possible values for $2\theta$ are between $180^\circ - 37^\circ = 143^\circ$ and $180^\circ - 30^\circ = 150^\circ$.

Thus, the possible ranges for $2\theta$ are:

$30^\circ \le 2\theta \le 37^\circ \Rightarrow 15^\circ \le \theta \le 18.5^\circ$

$143^\circ \le 2\theta \le 150^\circ \Rightarrow 71.5^\circ \le \theta \le 75^\circ$

The minimum angle of projection is $15^\circ$ and the maximum angle is $75^\circ$.

The forbidden interval of angles for a successful shot is between $18.5^\circ$ and $71.5^\circ$.

The width of this forbidden interval is $71.5^\circ - 18.5^\circ = 53^\circ$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: During a soccer practice session, the football is positioned at the centre of the field $40$ yards from the $10 \text{ ft}$ high goalposts. A goal is attempted by kicking the football at a speed of $64 \text{ ft/s}$ at an angle of $45^\circ$ to the horizontal. Will the ball reach the goal post?
options: Yes, it reaches the goalposts at a height of $9.5 \text{ ft}$.
No, it passes over the goalposts at a height of $12.5 \text{ ft}$.
Yes, it reaches the goalposts at a height of $7.5 \text{ ft}$.
No, it lands on the ground before reaching the goalposts.
questions.solution:
Given, initial speed $u = 64 \text{ ft/s}$

Angle of projection $\theta = 45^\circ$

Horizontal distance to goalposts $x = 40 \text{ yards} = 40 \times 3 = 120 \text{ ft}$

Acceleration due to gravity $g = 32 \text{ ft/s}^2$

The equation of trajectory is given by:

$y = x \tan \theta - \dfrac{g x^2}{2 u^2 \cos^2 \theta}$

Substituting the values:

$y = 120 \tan 45^\circ - \dfrac{32 \times (120)^2}{2 \times (64)^2 \cos^2 45^\circ}$

$y = 120 (1) - \dfrac{32 \times 14400}{2 \times 4096 \times \dfrac{1}{2}}$

$y = 120 - \dfrac{14400}{128}$

$y = 120 - 112.5 = 7.5 \text{ ft}$

Since the height of the ball is $7.5 \text{ ft}$, which is less than the goalpost height of $10 \text{ ft}$, the ball reaches the goalposts at a height of $7.5 \text{ ft}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A river $400 \text{ m}$ wide flows at a rate of $2.0 \text{ m/s}$. A boat sails at a velocity of $10 \text{ m/s}$ with respect to the water, in a direction perpendicular to the river. Determine how far from the point directly opposite to the starting point the boat reaches the opposite bank, in meters.
questions.solution:
Let the width of the river be $w = 400 \text{ m}$.

The velocity of the boat with respect to the water is $v_{br} = 10 \text{ m/s}$.

Since the boat is steered perpendicular to the river flow, the time taken to cross the river is given by:

$t = \dfrac{w}{v_{br}} = \dfrac{400}{10} = 40 \text{ s}$

The river flows at a velocity of $v_r = 2.0 \text{ m/s}$.

The drift of the boat, which is the distance from the point directly opposite to the starting point, is calculated as:

$x = v_r \times t$

$x = 2.0 \times 40 = 80 \text{ m}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Consider the situation of the previous problem. The man has to reach the other shore at the point directly opposite to his starting point. If he reaches the other shore somewhere else, he has to walk down to this point. Find the minimum distance that he has to walk.
questions.solution: $\dfrac{2}{3} \text{ km}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A swimmer desires to cross a $500 \text{ m}$ wide river that is flowing at $5 \text{ km/h}$. His speed relative to the water is $3 \text{ km/h}$. Determine the shortest possible time in minutes to cross the river.
questions.solution:
Let the width of the river be $d = 500 \text{ m} = 0.5 \text{ km}$.

The velocity of the swimmer relative to the water is $v_{s} = 3 \text{ km/h}$.

The velocity of the river flow is $v_{r} = 5 \text{ km/h}$.

To cross the river in the shortest possible time, the swimmer must head exactly perpendicular to the river flow. The component of the swimmer's velocity along the width of the river is maximum in this case and is equal to $v_{s}$.

The shortest time taken to cross the river is given by:

$t_{\text{min}} = \dfrac{d}{v_{s}}$

$t_{\text{min}} = \dfrac{0.5}{3} \text{ hours}$

Converting the time into minutes:

$t_{\text{min}} = \dfrac{0.5}{3} \times 60 \text{ minutes} = 10 \text{ minutes}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An aeroplane is required to travel from a point $A$ to another point $B$, located $500 \text{ km}$ away due $30^\circ$ east of north. A wind blows due north at a speed of $20 \text{ m/s}$, and the air-speed of the plane is $150 \text{ m/s}$. Determine the direction in which the pilot should head the plane to reach point $B$.
questions.solution:
Let $\vec{v}_{p}$ be the velocity of the plane with respect to the ground, $\vec{v}_{w}$ be the velocity of the wind, and $\vec{v}_{pw}$ be the velocity of the plane with respect to the wind.

We are given that $\vec{v}_{p} = \vec{v}_{pw} + \vec{v}_{w}$.

Let the north direction be along the y-axis ($\hat{j}$) and the east direction be along the x-axis ($\hat{i}$).

The wind velocity is $\vec{v}_{w} = 20 \hat{j}$.

Let the plane head at an angle $\alpha$ east of north. The velocity of the plane with respect to the wind is:

$\vec{v}_{pw} = 150 \sin \alpha \hat{i} + 150 \cos \alpha \hat{j}$

The velocity of the plane with respect to the ground becomes:

$\vec{v}_{p} = 150 \sin \alpha \hat{i} + (150 \cos \alpha + 20) \hat{j}$

For the plane to travel along the line $AB$, which is $30^\circ$ east of north, the resultant velocity $\vec{v}_{p}$ must also be directed $30^\circ$ east of north. Thus, the ratio of its x-component to its y-component must be $\tan 30^\circ$:

$\dfrac{150 \sin \alpha}{150 \cos \alpha + 20} = \tan 30^\circ = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{3}}$

Rearranging the equation:

$150 \sqrt{3} \sin \alpha = 150 \cos \alpha + 20$

$150 (\sqrt{3} \sin \alpha - \cos \alpha) = 20$

$\sqrt{3} \sin \alpha - \cos \alpha = \dfrac{20}{150} = \dfrac{2}{15}$

Dividing both sides by $2$:

$\dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \sin \alpha - \dfrac{1}{2} \cos \alpha = \dfrac{1}{15}$

Using the trigonometric identity $\sin A \cos B - \cos A \sin B = \sin(A - B)$:

$\sin \alpha \cos 30^\circ - \cos \alpha \sin 30^\circ = \dfrac{1}{15}$

$\sin(\alpha - 30^\circ) = \dfrac{1}{15}$

$\alpha - 30^\circ = \sin^{-1}\left(\dfrac{1}{15}\right)$

Since $\alpha$ is the angle east of north and the line $AB$ is $30^\circ$ east of north, the difference $\alpha - 30^\circ$ represents the angle east of the line $AB$.

Therefore, the pilot should head $\sin^{-1}\left(\dfrac{1}{15}\right)$ east of the line $AB$.

Answer: $\sin^{-1}\left(\dfrac{1}{15}\right)$ east of the line $AB$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An aeroplane is required to travel from a point $A$ to another point $B$, located $500 \text{ km}$ away due $30^\circ$ east of north. A wind blows due north at a speed of $20 \text{ m/s}$, and the air-speed of the plane is $150 \text{ m/s}$. Determine the time taken by the plane to go from $A$ to $B$ in minutes.
questions.solution:
Let $\vec{v}_p$ be the velocity of the plane with respect to the ground, $\vec{v}_w$ be the velocity of the wind, and $\vec{v}_{pw}$ be the velocity of the plane with respect to the wind.

We know that $\vec{v}_p = \vec{v}_{pw} + \vec{v}_w$, which can be rearranged as $\vec{v}_{pw} = \vec{v}_p - \vec{v}_w$.

The direction of $\vec{v}_p$ is $30^\circ$ east of north, and the direction of $\vec{v}_w$ is due north. The angle between $\vec{v}_p$ and $\vec{v}_w$ is $30^\circ$.

Using the cosine rule for the vector triangle:

$v_{pw}^2 = v_p^2 + v_w^2 - 2 v_p v_w \cos(30^\circ)$

Substituting the given values ($v_{pw} = 150 \text{ m/s}$, $v_w = 20 \text{ m/s}$):

$150^2 = v_p^2 + 20^2 - 2 v_p (20) \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$

$22500 = v_p^2 + 400 - 20\sqrt{3} v_p$

$v_p^2 - 20\sqrt{3} v_p - 22100 = 0$

Solving the quadratic equation for $v_p$ (taking the positive root):

$v_p = \dfrac{20\sqrt{3} + \sqrt{(20\sqrt{3})^2 - 4(1)(-22100)}}{2}$

$v_p = \dfrac{20\sqrt{3} + \sqrt{1200 + 88400}}{2} = 10\sqrt{3} + \sqrt{22400}$

$v_p = 10(\sqrt{3} + \sqrt{224}) \approx 10(1.732 + 14.967) = 166.99 \text{ m/s}$

The distance to be covered is $d = 500 \text{ km} = 500 \times 10^3 \text{ m}$.

Time taken $t = \dfrac{d}{v_p} = \dfrac{500000}{166.99} \approx 2994.2 \text{ s}$

Converting the time into minutes:

$t = \dfrac{2994.2}{60} \approx 49.9 \text{ min}$

Answer: $49.9$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Suppose $A$ and $B$ in the previous problem change their positions in such a way that the line joining them becomes perpendicular to the direction of wind while maintaining the separation $x$. What will be the time lag $B$ finds between seeing and hearing the drum beating by $A$?
questions.solution: $\dfrac{x}{\sqrt{v^2 - u^2}}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Six particles positioned at the corners of a regular hexagon of side $a$ travel at a constant speed $v$. Each particle continually maintains a direction towards the particle at the next corner. Determine the time it will take for the particles to meet each other.
questions.solution:
The particles are located at the vertices of a regular hexagon. The velocity of each particle is directed towards the adjacent particle.

The angle between the line joining two adjacent particles and the velocity of the second particle is the exterior angle of the regular hexagon, which is $60^{\circ}$.

The relative velocity of approach between any two adjacent particles is:

$v_{app} = v - v \cos(60^{\circ}) = v - \dfrac{v}{2} = \dfrac{v}{2}$

The initial distance between the adjacent particles is $a$. The time taken for the particles to meet is the ratio of the initial distance to the relative velocity of approach:

$t = \dfrac{a}{v_{app}} = \dfrac{a}{\dfrac{v}{2}} = \dfrac{2a}{v}$

Alternatively, the distance of each particle from the center of the hexagon is $a$. The angle between the velocity vector and the position vector from the center is $60^{\circ}$.

The component of velocity directed towards the center is:

$v_{r} = v \cos(60^{\circ}) = \dfrac{v}{2}$

Since the particles meet at the center, the time taken is:

$t = \dfrac{a}{v_{r}} = \dfrac{2a}{v}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Standing on a long railroad car, a boy throws a ball straight upwards. The car travels on a horizontal road with an acceleration of $1 \text{ m/s}^2$, and the projection velocity in the vertical direction is $9.8 \text{ m/s}$. Find how far behind the boy the ball will fall on the car, in m.
questions.solution:
The time of flight of the ball is given by $T = \dfrac{2u_y}{g}$.

Substituting $u_y = 9.8 \text{ m/s}$ and $g = 9.8 \text{ m/s}^2$, we get $T = \dfrac{2 \times 9.8}{9.8} = 2 \text{ s}$.

In the horizontal direction, the ball moves with a constant velocity equal to the velocity of the car at the instant of projection.

The car accelerates horizontally with $a = 1 \text{ m/s}^2$.

The relative horizontal acceleration of the ball with respect to the car is $a_{rel} = -1 \text{ m/s}^2$.

The initial relative horizontal velocity is $u_{rel} = 0$.

The horizontal displacement of the ball relative to the car is $s = u_{rel}T + \dfrac{1}{2}a_{rel}T^2$.

Substituting the values, $s = 0 + \dfrac{1}{2}(-1)(2)^2 = -2 \text{ m}$.

The ball will fall $2 \text{ m}$ behind the boy.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A swimmer desires to cross a $500 \text{ m}$ wide river that is flowing at $5 \text{ km/h}$. His speed relative to the water is $3 \text{ km/h}$. If he heads in a direction making an angle $\theta$ with the flow, determine the time he takes to cross the river.
questions.solution:
Width of the river, $d = 500 \text{ m} = 0.5 \text{ km}$

Velocity of the swimmer relative to water, $v_{sr} = 3 \text{ km/h}$

Angle made by the swimmer with the direction of river flow $= \theta$

Component of the swimmer's velocity perpendicular to the river flow $= v_{sr} \sin\theta = 3 \sin\theta \text{ km/h}$

Time taken to cross the river, $t = \dfrac{d}{v_{sr} \sin\theta}$

$t = \dfrac{0.5}{3 \sin\theta} \text{ hours}$

$t = \dfrac{0.5 \times 60}{3 \sin\theta} \text{ minutes} = \dfrac{10}{\sin\theta} \text{ minutes}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two friends $A$ and $B$ are standing a distance $x$ apart in an open field and wind is blowing from $A$ to $B$. $A$ beats a drum, and $B$ hears the sound $t_1$ time after seeing the event. $A$ and $B$ swap their positions and the experiment is repeated. This time, $B$ hears the drum $t_2$ time after seeing the event. Determine the velocity of sound in still air $v$ and the velocity of wind $u$. Neglect the time light takes to travel between the friends.
questions.solution:
Let $v$ be the velocity of sound in still air and $u$ be the velocity of wind.

In the first case, the wind blows from $A$ to $B$. The velocity of sound relative to the ground is $v + u$.

The time taken for sound to travel distance $x$ is $t_1 = \dfrac{x}{v + u}$.

$v + u = \dfrac{x}{t_1}$

In the second case, $A$ and $B$ swap positions. The sound now travels from the new position of $A$ to the new position of $B$, which is opposite to the direction of the wind.

The velocity of sound relative to the ground is $v - u$.

The time taken for sound to travel distance $x$ is $t_2 = \dfrac{x}{v - u}$.

$v - u = \dfrac{x}{t_2}$

Adding the two equations:

$(v + u) + (v - u) = \dfrac{x}{t_1} + \dfrac{x}{t_2}$

$2v = x \left( \dfrac{1}{t_1} + \dfrac{1}{t_2} \right)$

$v = \dfrac{x}{2} \left( \dfrac{1}{t_1} + \dfrac{1}{t_2} \right)$

Subtracting the second equation from the first:

$(v + u) - (v - u) = \dfrac{x}{t_1} - \dfrac{x}{t_2}$

$2u = x \left( \dfrac{1}{t_1} - \dfrac{1}{t_2} \right)$

$u = \dfrac{x}{2} \left( \dfrac{1}{t_1} - \dfrac{1}{t_2} \right)$
